title: Chapter 4: 42 Beats Per Minute
content:
```
APPLE HEALTH EXPORT — HEART RATE
User: Lucy Chen
Device: Apple Watch Series 9 (A2980)
Export Date: October 19, 2026

OCTOBER 15, 2026
Resting HR: 62 bpm
Walking HR: 84 bpm
Range: 58–112 bpm
SpO2: 97–98%
Notes: No anomalies detected.

OCTOBER 16, 2026
03:14:22 — HR 61 bpm, SpO2 98%
03:19:07 — HR 58 bpm, SpO2 97%
03:22:41 — HR 42 bpm, SpO2 91% ⚠️
03:23:58 — HR 42 bpm, SpO2 79% ⚠️
03:24:33 — HR 44 bpm, SpO2 67% ⚠️ CRITICAL
03:25:01 — HR 180 bpm, SpO2 67% ⚠️ CRITICAL
03:25:47 — HR 176 bpm, SpO2 71%
03:27:14 — HR 154 bpm, SpO2 82%
03:28:59 — HR 138 bpm, SpO2 89%
03:30:02 — HR 112 bpm, SpO2 94%
03:31:45 — HR 98 bpm, SpO2 94%
Range: 42–180 bpm
SpO2 Range: 67–98%
Notes: Irregular rhythm detected. Sudden tachycardic event.
Blood oxygen recovery inconsistent with physiological norms.

OCTOBER 17, 2026
Resting HR: 48 bpm ⚠️
Walking HR: 64 bpm
Range: 46–89 bpm
SpO2: 93–95%
Notes: Resting heart rate deviation of -14 bpm from baseline.
Persistent mild hypoxia.

OCTOBER 18, 2026
Resting HR: 48 bpm ⚠️
Range: 47–127 bpm (transient spikes inconsistent with activity)
SpO2: 92–95%
Notes: New baseline established. Continued deviation.
Anomalous spike clusters detected — see detailed log.
```
The warehouse had no right being this cold in October.

Maya sat cross-legged on the concrete floor, her legs long past the pins-and-needles stage and into something closer to numbness. Six hours. Six hours since Chloe had cracked the iCloud and started the download, and Maya hadn't moved from this spot except to pee twice and pour coffee she no longer tasted. The mug beside her had gone cold three hours ago — she could smell it, that flat, bitter staleness that cold coffee becomes when you've forgotten it exists.
She didn't care about the coffee. She cared about the graph on the screen.

The blood oxygen curve. SpO2 for Lucy Chen, October 16th, 3:22 AM. The line fell like a body off a cliff — 98, 91, 79, 67. Sixty-seven percent. Maya had taken enough first-aid courses to know what that number meant. Below 80, you're looking at cognitive impairment. Below 70, organ damage. Below 65, you die.

Lucy had hit 67 and then — what? The data said she spiked to 180. Her heart had jammed into overdrive like a compressor trying to reinflate something collapsed. And the blood oxygen came back. 67 to 71 to 82 to 89 to 94. In under three minutes.
Maya's voice came out flat, mechanical, reading the data out loud because silence felt dangerous: "You don't drop to 67% SpO2 and survive without intervention. Someone brought her back."
Chloe didn't look up from her keyboard. Her fingers kept moving, pulling data apart in another window. "Or something brought her back."

The word hung. *Something.* Not someone. Something. Maya felt the metallic tang at the back of her throat that she'd started to recognize as her own fear — sharp, copperish, like pennies left in rain. She'd tasted it first in Chloe's car two nights ago, reading the Lumina blood-type filter code. She tasted it now, stronger.

Her eyes tracked left on the screen to the October 17 data. Resting heart rate: 48. Lucy's baseline was 62 — Maya knew this because Lucy had joked about it constantly, how her resting heart rate was proof she was "calm and spiritually advanced," which was the funniest thing Maya had ever heard from a woman who once threw a smoothie at a Uber driver.
Forty-eight wasn't Lucy's baseline. Something had changed her body.
"Play it again."

Maya was standing now, leaning over Chloe's shoulder at the main station. The cold floor had gotten to her, or maybe it was the need to move, to do something with her body that wasn't just sitting and staring. Pro Tools was open on the screen — Maya's idea, her domain. She'd asked Chloe to export the heart rate values as a CSV, then she'd mapped each bpm reading to a frequency. 60 bpm became 60 Hz. 180 became 180 Hz. Crude, but she didn't need fidelity. She needed pattern.

The first playback had been chaos — a wash of mid-range tones that sounded like someone dragging a bow across a detuned cello. Noise. But Maya had been working with audio for years. She knew what noise felt like, and this wasn't it. Noise was random. This had weight in places. Clusters.

She isolated the anomalous spikes — the bpm values that didn't match Lucy's activity state, the ones that appeared while she was at rest. She stripped out the baseline. What remained were short bursts and longer durations, spaced at intervals that weren't random. She'd seen this before, in a documentary about prisoners of war who communicated by tapping on pipes.
Short. Long. Short short. Long long.
Morse code.
"Play it slower," Maya said. Her mouth was dry.

Chloe adjusted the playback rate. The tones dropped in pitch, stretched out, and the pattern became audible — a rhythm that was unmistakably intentional. Dots and dashes. Someone was speaking through heart rate.
*Lucy was speaking through heart rate.*

Chloe grabbed a pen from the chaos of her desk — the hard plastic barrel pressing grooves into her grip — and pulled a receipt from a coffee shop, flipping it over. She started writing. Maya called out the lengths as they played.
"Short. Long. Short-short. Long-long."
Dot dash. Dot dot. Dash dash.
A. E. T.

Chloe's handwriting was a jagged scrawl across the thermal paper, pressing hard enough to leave impressions. They worked without speaking except for the calls, two women hunched over a screen in a cold warehouse decoding a heartbeat, and the absurdity of it — the *miracle* of it — didn't land until Chloe read it back:
"A-E-T-E-R-N-A. Space. S-U-B. Space. B. Space. A-L-I-V-E. Space. L-U-C-Y."
AETERNA SUB B ALIVE LUCY.

Maya's hands were shaking. It was the same message from the podcast audio — the 0.7 Hz breathing pattern they'd decoded in this same warehouse forty-eight hours ago. Confirmation. Independent confirmation. Lucy was alive, and Lucy was under Aeterna, Sub-level B, and Lucy had found a way to say so using nothing but the rhythm of her own pulse.

"There's more," Chloe said. She hadn't stopped the playback. The tones continued — a second transmission, sent the next day, October 17th. The spikes were subtler this time, the deviations smaller. Lucy had learned. She was refining her technique, compressing more information into tighter windows, tapping out signals with her own cardiac muscle while whoever held her presumably didn't notice a smartwatch was still transmitting.
It took them four minutes to decode the second message. Chloe read it aloud, and her voice cracked on the third word:
"8-4-7. WOMEN. CAN'T. ESCAPE. BRING. HELP."
Eight hundred and forty-seven.
Maya went silent.

The warehouse hummed around her — Chloe's servers, the refrigerator in the kitchenette, the fluorescent light above the workbench that flickered every eleven seconds, a rhythm Maya had unconsciously been counting. Thirty seconds passed. Maybe forty. She stood perfectly still, her jaw locked, her fingernails pressing crescents into her palms.
Then she spoke, very quietly: "She's not just surviving. She's been COUNTING. She's been gathering intel while chained to a wall."

She said it the way you say something when the alternative is screaming. The image was so vivid it made her dizzy — Lucy in the dark, under a mountain, keeping a running tally of every woman they brought in, committing numbers to memory, waiting for the moment she could tap out a message through her own goddamn heart. While Maya had been scrolling Instagram and assuming Lucy was on a bender. While Maya had been *unfollowing* her.

Chloe was looking at her. Not at the screen. At Maya. There was something in her expression that Maya didn't expect — not pity, not the careful therapeutic sympathy Maya had gotten from everyone else since Lucy disappeared. This was something rawer. Respect. Not for Maya.
For Lucy.
"Your friend," Chloe said slowly, "is harder than both of us combined."
Maya nodded. She couldn't speak. She didn't trust her voice.

Chloe's fingers were flying again, but different now — driven, not methodical. The Lumina user database she'd extracted from Aeterna's AWS instance was open in three windows, cross-referenced against the social media accounts of the 847 women Lucy had counted.
The pattern emerged in under an hour.

Twenty-three women had disappeared in the past eighteen months. Not all 847 — the others were still "active," whatever that meant in Lumina's sterilized clinical language. But twenty-three had gone dark. Their social media accounts had posted final updates — breezy, wellness-coded messages about digital detox retreats and personal health sabbaticals. "Taking time to reconnect with myself." "Off-grid for a while — text if you need me." "Gratitude for this journey inward."
Every single post — all twenty-three — had been crafted and scheduled by the same PR firm.
Whitby Communications.
Maya read the name and felt something cold settle in her sternum. "That's not a coincidence."
"No." Chloe's voice was tight. "That's a cleanup crew."

A PR firm that erased women. Made them vanish palatably. Gave their friends and families a narrative that explained the silence, the absence, the uncanny feeling that someone you loved had been replaced by a scheduling algorithm. *Digital detox.* Maya wanted to break something. She could smell the old coffee and the ozone from Chloe's servers and underneath it all, the faint iron tang of the concrete floor, mineral and cold, like a basement. Like a sub-level.

She thought about Lucy's heart rate. Forty-eight bpm. A new normal. Something had been done to Lucy's body — something that changed her cardiac baseline, that dropped her oxygen saturation to a chronic mild hypoxia, that made her blood less valuable to whatever was taking it and more valuable to whatever was *keeping* it. Lucy was alive because they needed her alive. And she was using her captivity to count.
Eight hundred and forty-seven women.
October 18th. Late evening. The most recent data in the export.

Maya spotted it herself — a longer sequence than the previous two messages, transmitted over a period of nearly two hours according to the timestamps. Lucy had been patient. She'd spaced the spikes further apart, buried them in what looked like normal circadian variation. To anyone not looking for Morse code, it would read as mild sleep disturbance.

It took them twenty minutes to decode. Chloe's pen had given way to a stylus on her tablet, and they worked side by side, calling out letters, double-checking, erasing, reconfirming. The warehouse was silent except for the hum and their voices — two people reading the words of a woman they couldn't reach, transmitted through the only channel she had left.
T-H-E-Y. M-O-V-E. U-S. I-P-O. D-A-Y. A-L-L. O-F. U-S. H-U-R-R-Y.
THEY MOVE US IPO DAY ALL OF US HURRY.
Maya read it twice. Three times. The words didn't change.

IPO day. All of them. They were going to move 847 women on IPO day. Whatever Aeterna was — whatever they had built under the streets of San Francisco, in Sub-level B, in the dark where Lucy Chen was tapping out SOS signals with her own cardiac rhythm — they were going to relocate it. Liquidate the assets. Make the women disappear the way the other twenty-three had disappeared, but all at once, all together, all at the same moment the world was looking up at the stock price and cheering.
"When is the IPO?" Maya whispered.
Chloe's fingers moved. A browser opened. She typed *Aeterna Corp IPO date* into a search bar and hit enter.
The results loaded. Chloe's face went white.
"November 15th." She swallowed. "That's 28 days."
Twenty-eight days. Four weeks. Less than a month to find 847 women in a building they couldn't locate, held by a company they couldn't fight, in a system designed to make them invisible.

Maya stared at the screen. The Apple Watch data glowed back at her — Lucy's heart rate, Lucy's oxygen, Lucy's fraying persistence encoded in the surges and rests of a muscle she had trained to speak. Somewhere under San Francisco, Lucy Chen was keeping count in the dark. Waiting. Believing — because she had to believe — that someone was listening.
Maya felt her jaw set. Her hands stopped shaking.
Twenty-eight days. She'd burn every hour.
She looked at Chloe and saw the same calculation happening behind her eyes — the frantic arithmetic of 847 divided by 28, the impossible logistics, the certainty that they would try anyway.

Lucy had tapped out Hurry. She'd used the only language she had left, the only instrument they hadn't taken from her, the one that was literally beating inside her chest. She'd said hurry, and she'd meant it, and she was right.
Maya reached for her cold coffee and drank it anyway.
*28 days.*
